Any thoughts that we had that the people out there in rural Missouri are rude just vanished.
You're very media savvy.
And I did want to ask, though, what's the fallout going to be?
Is the sheriff, the county sheriff, still investigating?
The investigation's complete.
The sheriff's department, the highway patrol and the FBI did a coordinated investigation, and it took a long time because there were so many people whose civil rights were violated.
But they have compiled a report and turned it over to the U.S.
attorney, and they will not say anything until the charges have been filed.
There'll be no further word now until the charges have actually been filed.

Linda Trest, great story.
Thank you very much for being with us today.
Thanks again for having me.
Linda Trest, a staff writer for the Gasconade County Republican with us today from the studios of KWMU, our member station in St. Louis.
You can find links to several of her articles on our blog at npr.org/blogofthenation.
This is Talk of the Nation from NPR News.
I'm Neal Conan in Washington.
Anyone who's been caught in traffic on I-95 - any other interstate, for that matter - may think that our next guests may have breathed in too much car exhaust.
Stan Posner and Sandra Phillips-Posner don't drive along I-95 because they have to.
They do it - over and over again - because they like to.
Along the way they've learned everything you may need to know, and much that you probably don't care to know at all about I-95.
They're authors of the book ""Drive I-95: Exit by Exit Info, Maps, History and Trivia.
They join us from the studios of the CBC in Montreal.
Thanks so much for being with us.
You're welcome.
You're welcome.
So, how long have you been I-95 road warriors?
Seven years now.
And why?
What's so fascinating?
People don't realize how much fun they could have every day.
They think they get in their car and they have this, oh, we've got to make it there after so many hours.
How are we ever going to do this?
And what we tell them is you're going to take a break every day.
So take a break where you can enjoy it and have something to look forward to every day.
What about road food?
Yum.
Well, give us some particulars, specifics if you could.
There are so many family restaurants - and we're talking right on exits.
We only go about...
We go, like, half a mile to a mile off of the exits on each side.
Like, to the second traffic light, so no one's going to get lost.
Can you tell us a couple?
Yeah.
I'm going to mention in - I'm thinking of Florence, South Carolina, where we stop in for that potato soup and the toast - what is it - a croissant with a...
With honey on it.
Honey on it.
It's called Percy and Willie's.
Okay?
And it's right on the exit.
Wait, one second.
Our favorite, the bottom of the New Jersey Turnpike, guys, we have a favorite Italian restaurant.
Yeah, it's called Italia.
It's right on Exit 3 on the Turnpike.
And we love the calzone there.
It's filed with this goo.
We like the one that has the gooey cheeses and fresh ham.
And they make this fabulous marinara sauce that you pour all over it.
It's a family recipe that they have not divulged to us yet.

There's actually one very close to you - the bottom of Baltimore, Exit 50.
It's called Polock Johnny's.
Back on the bus, visitors scroll through their tablet computers, looking over photos and taking down notes.
But, more than ideas for store layout or amazement at our portion sizes, these visitors are coming away with a different picture of America.
Algerian pastry chef Djaouhar Nawel says that food helps shape this picture because it's so basic to who we are, even from the very beginning.
(Through translator) Like a woman delivers a baby, the very first thing he'll be looking for is food.
And when we are traveling around visiting other countries, first thing we are looking for is food.
And when you find American food in all its diversity, you find America.
Through food you can understand the cultures.
Ashraf Abdou runs the Egyptian Chefs Association.
And with a culture, you can understand the people.
In our countries it's a negative idea about the Americans because they saw only that is in the media.
They didn't know the people themselves.
And the hope is that getting to know people and their culture sets the table for a very basic level of diplomacy - getting a taste of who we are.
For NPR News, I'm Deena Prichep, in Portland Ore.
Gentlemen, welcome.
Hello, thanks.
Thank you.
So Marcus, let's start with you as a historian.
You've just heard my conversation with Robert Moses.
He was already an activist dealing with civil rights when Vietnam started.
How would you characterize him?
Would you consider him an anti-war activist?
He did leave the country.
So it was a very active movement, and one that unfortunately is not really focused upon, you know, very much.
But it's somewhat complex in that you're not going to be able to define one central reason why people were against the war or one central avenue that they took.
Marcus, when you - you're a historian.
When you look at African- Americans and the military and an opposition to military action, especially war, what have you seen over the course of history?
Is there - are there any trends that we can see?

And what happens is if you look throughout American history, African-Americans, and in particular minorities, have always questioned themselves in relation to this whole issue, because if, in fact, I can better myself in some way, then it's a worthwhile endeavor.
But if not, if my situation is not going to change by virtue of my sacrifice or my commitment, then how am I going to gain from this experience?
And so you're going to find that throughout, you know, our(ph) American history.
